title: Alocasia Polly Care Guide: Grow a Thriving Plant
excerpt: Learn everything you need to know about Alocasia Polly care, from watering and light to humidity and common problems. This complete guide will help your African Mask plant thrive indoors.
content:
Alocasia Polly Care Guide: Everything You Need to Know

With its dramatic arrow-shaped leaves and striking white veins, Alocasia Polly (also known as the African Mask plant or Alocasia amazonica 'Polly') is one of the most eye-catching houseplants you can grow. It looks like it belongs in a tropical rainforest — because it basically does. The good news? You can recreate those conditions right in your home.

This Alocasia Polly care guide covers everything from light and watering to humidity, soil, and troubleshooting common problems. Whether you're a first-time Alocasia owner or looking to level up your care routine, you're in the right place.

Quick Care Overview

  • Light: Bright, indirect light
  • Water: When the top 1–2 inches of soil are dry
  • Humidity: 60% or higher
  • Temperature: 65–85°F (18–29°C)
  • Soil: Well-draining, airy mix
  • Fertilizer: Monthly during spring and summer
  • Toxicity: Toxic to pets and humans

Light Requirements

Alocasia Polly thrives in bright, indirect light. Think of a spot near an east- or north-facing window, or a few feet back from a south- or west-facing window. Direct sunlight will scorch those gorgeous leaves, leaving unsightly brown patches that won't recover.

Low light is another problem — in dim conditions, your Alocasia will grow slowly, lose its vibrant color, and become more susceptible to root rot. If your home doesn't get much natural light, a grow light placed 12–18 inches above the plant can make a huge difference.

Signs Your Light Level Is Off

  • Scorched, brown patches: Too much direct sun
  • Pale, washed-out leaves: Too much light intensity
  • Leggy growth or drooping: Not enough light

Watering Your Alocasia Polly

Watering is where most people go wrong with Alocasia Polly. This plant hates both extremes — it doesn't want to sit in soggy soil, but it also doesn't appreciate being left bone dry for too long.

The golden rule: water when the top 1–2 inches of soil feel dry. Stick your finger into the soil to check, or use a moisture meter for more accuracy. During the growing season (spring and summer), you may be watering every 7–10 days. In winter, cut back significantly as the plant enters a rest period and water uptake slows.

Always use room-temperature water, and if possible, use filtered or rainwater. Alocasia Polly can be sensitive to the fluoride and chlorine in tap water, which may cause brown leaf tips over time.

How to Water Properly

  1. Check soil moisture before watering
  2. Water thoroughly until it drains from the bottom
  3. Empty the saucer after 30 minutes to prevent waterlogging
  4. Never let the plant sit in standing water

Humidity and Temperature

This is where Alocasia Polly care gets serious. As a tropical plant, it demands high humidity — ideally 60% or above. Most homes sit around 30–50%, which is often not enough to keep this plant truly happy.

Here's how to boost humidity effectively:

  • Use a humidifier: The most reliable method, especially in winter
  • Group plants together: Plants release moisture through transpiration, raising the humidity around them
  • Place a pebble tray with water beneath the pot: As the water evaporates, it increases local humidity
  • Move it to a bathroom or kitchen: These rooms naturally have higher humidity

Skip the misting — it can encourage fungal issues and doesn't raise humidity in any meaningful way.

Temperature-wise, keep your Alocasia Polly between 65–85°F (18–29°C). Keep it away from cold drafts, air conditioning vents, and heating units. Temperatures below 60°F can shock the plant and trigger dormancy or leaf drop.

Soil and Potting

Alocasia Polly needs a well-draining, airy soil mix that retains some moisture without becoming waterlogged. A standard quality potting mix is usually too dense on its own. Try mixing:

  • 50% high-quality potting mix
  • 25% perlite
  • 25% orchid bark mix or coco coir

This combination allows roots to breathe, drains excess water quickly, and still holds enough moisture to keep the plant hydrated between waterings. You might also enjoy our guide on choosing the best soil for tropical houseplants if you want to dive deeper into soil science.

Repot your Alocasia Polly every 1–2 years, or when you see roots circling the bottom of the pot or poking out of drainage holes. Always choose a pot only 1–2 inches larger than the current one — too large a pot increases the risk of overwatering.

Fertilizing

Feed your Alocasia Polly once a month during spring and summer with a balanced liquid fertilizer diluted to half strength. A 20-20-20 NPK formula works well. Avoid fertilizing in fall and winter when the plant is resting — over-fertilizing during dormancy can cause salt buildup and root damage.

Common Problems and How to Fix Them

Yellow Leaves

Yellowing leaves are usually a sign of overwatering or poor drainage. Check your soil moisture and adjust your watering schedule. If root rot has set in, you may need to repot and trim any mushy roots.

Brown Leaf Tips

Brown tips typically point to low humidity, underwatering, or fluoride sensitivity. Boost humidity, switch to filtered water, and check that you're not letting the soil dry out completely.

Drooping Leaves

Drooping can signal both underwatering and overwatering — always check the soil first. Sudden drooping after a move or temperature change can also be stress-related. Give the plant time to adjust before making changes.

Pests

Alocasia Polly is prone to spider mites, mealybugs, and scale, especially in low humidity. Inspect leaves regularly, especially the undersides. Treat infestations early with neem oil or insecticidal soap. For a deeper dive, check out our article on identifying and treating common houseplant pests.

Dormancy: What to Expect in Winter

It's completely normal for Alocasia Polly to slow down or even lose leaves in winter. This is dormancy, not death. Reduce watering, stop fertilizing, and keep it warm. New growth will return in spring as light levels increase.

Is Alocasia Polly Right for You?

Alocasia Polly is a rewarding plant for those willing to meet its needs for humidity, bright indirect light, and careful watering. It's not a beginner's plant in the traditional sense, but with the right setup, it's absolutely manageable — even for newer plant parents. If you love the look but want something a bit more forgiving, explore our guide to easy tropical houseplants for some great alternatives.

Get the conditions right, and this stunning plant will reward you with bold, architectural foliage that transforms any indoor space.

Frequently Asked Questions

How often should I water Alocasia Polly?

Water when the top 1–2 inches of soil feel dry, roughly every 7–10 days during spring and summer. Cut back significantly in winter as the plant enters a rest period.

What kind of light does Alocasia Polly need?

Alocasia Polly thrives in bright, indirect light, such as near an east- or north-facing window. Avoid direct sunlight, which will scorch the leaves, and low light, which causes slow growth and root rot risk.

What humidity level does Alocasia Polly need?

Alocasia Polly needs humidity of 60% or higher. Since most homes sit around 30–50%, using a humidifier or grouping plants together is recommended to meet its tropical requirements.

Is Alocasia Polly toxic to pets?

Yes, Alocasia Polly is toxic to both pets and humans and should be kept out of reach of animals and children.

Why does my Alocasia Polly have brown tips?

Brown leaf tips are often caused by sensitivity to fluoride and chlorine in tap water. Switching to filtered or rainwater at room temperature can help prevent this issue.
